item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kind of magical to see something from being an idea to being a draft to being an article and succeeding at it. This is what I call: The Good Stuff. Your story here is unique, and the way you tell it is unique, and the dialogue is engaging, and the anomaly is novel, and it's all just very good. +1 from me, I hope to see more from you in the future my dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing most in this thread. Really liked this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very well-done. An original premise executed skillfully.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I was turned off by D-919454's rambly speech, thinking it distracted from the anomaly and beating around the bush as to what made it special, but more I read the more I realized that the rambly nature of the dialogue was the point. Given the themes of death and loneliness, the rambly dialogue is D-919454's life of sorrow and fear, not knowing what comes next, before coming to a peaceful conclusion. The dialogue was well-written enough that I was engaged in the man's story, and when he mentioned Beatrice, it finally made sense.

+1. Good shit.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
I fixed the formatting error I saw in the interview.

Thank you so much for reading, I'm glad this had that impact on someone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed it! Thank you for letting me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a heads-up, you can remove the "#" before the "2" on the ACS component to get a proper display.

This one really hit different. My entire life has been centered around work, where I can succeed, where I fail, what I'm gonna do next, where I'm supposed to end up- and often times I think to myself, "What happens after?" And what happens after is just more and more work until I can't work anymore. And once I can't work anymore, I have no place.

In that regard, I see myself in this D-Class, with his fears of being put away and forgotten, neatly tucked into a little hole so he can be taken care of by nurses and never visited by his family except on holidays, or every few weekends if he's especially lucky. It hurts to see him struggle like this, and his dialogue portrays this perfectly.

I noticed some formatting errors in the interview log, but all in all an enthusiastic upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't leave someone alone if you can help it. Call your family, kids.

I have more than a few people to thank, and I'll thank them all individually. But most of all, thanks go out to DarkStuff and Cyvstvi13 for the greenlights, to Sonderance and cybersqyd for going through it with a fine-tooth comb. And also, to the many people in #thecritters that I pestered and honestly should've kept a list of. Which I will, for next time.
And of course, to the whole damn community for the chance.

See you next time.
